title: Chapter 27
text: Chapter Twenty - Four E ven before breaking into a sprint, Ronin knew there’d be no outrunning the storm. Their only hope was shelter. His processors worked in overdrive, poring over stored data in the search for a shelter somewhere nearby — a building, or a cave, or even a partially collapsed wall to hide behind . Lara clung to him, her face buried against his shoulder. The wind was strong at his back, pushing him forward, whipping her hair into his optics. If they’d had the chance to resupply before leaving Cheyenne, he might have located some sort of air filtration system for her. That would have diminished at least one of the dangers they were about to face . Lightning flashed, followed two and a half seconds later by a crack of thunder. Lara lifted her head and wadded his coat in her fists. “ Ronin !” “We’re going to be fine,” he said, amplifying his vocal synthesizer so she could hear him over the roaring wind . He ran up a hill into a copse of trees, several which were still living. If there were no alternatives, the ancient boughs would help break up the wind a little, but they wouldn’t provide adequate shelter for Lara. He should have kept their course closer to the old road. At least then, he might have located a culvert or something of the sort they could shelter within . Finally emerging from the trees, he stopped on the crest of the hill. The land before him leveled out into a wide, open expanse, littered with brush, weeds, brown grass, and dirt, only rising into another hill in the distance. Ronin swept his optics across the area, catching a contrast in color several hundred yards out. Buildings . Ronin worked his way down to the open ground. Several hundred pounds of gear — their clothing, ammunition, tools, food, water, and two hauls’ worth of scrap — in addition to Lara in his arms had his compensators and actuators operating well above their normal levels of strain. If she was jostled by his speed, she made no complaint . The only standing structure was a house, and as they neared, it was increasingly clear that time had not been kind to it. Paint was peeling off the wooden siding, and the siding itself had been so exposed to the elements that it, too, was peeling away from the underlying framework. The entire house leaned precariously to one side, three degrees off-center. The roof sagged, all the windows were shattered, and the front door hung on a single hinge . He slowed when they were within fifty feet of the building. It couldn’t be trusted to hold up to the punishment the dust storm was about to unleash. All the visual evidence suggested the house should have collapsed on itself years ago; it was a small miracle, perhaps, that it was standing, but that made Ronin no more willing to risk Lara’s life on it . Just north lay the remains of a tin-roofed barn. It looked to have folded neatly on top of itself, now blanketed with rust and tufts of grass . He turned to look east. The wall of dust stretched for countless miles, already flowing over the hills they’d just crossed. At best, they had three or four minutes before it hit them . Ronin carried Lara around the side, between the house and barn. The rusted carcass of a tractor jutted up out of the brush that was slowly overtaking it. Normally, he’d stop to pry open the engine housing and picked it for scrap; now, he walked past it without a second glance . The house would have to do. There was no other choice. He’d keep her shielded with his body, in case of a collapse … “There!” Lara pointed and wiggled free of his arms. “I’ve seen something like that before .” She raced ahead of him when he released his hold, toward a grass-covered mound about seventy feet away from the collapsed back porch. There was a single door on the side of the mound, its metal dulled by rust and caked-on dirt. She grasped the handle and pulled, throwing her weight into it, but it didn’t budge . Lara stepped aside when Ronin caught up. He wrapped his fingers around the handle and exerted gradual force; it was immediately clear that the door hadn’t seen any recent use. He increased the pressure, knowing it was more likely to break than to turn. The wind howled and thunder rolled across the heavens, loud enough for him to feel its soundwaves vibrate over his skin. The first dust particles hit him soon after . Lara twisted away from the wind, crouched, and raised her arms to shield herself. Time was up. The needed to get inside the house and hope for the best . With a metallic groan, the mechanism gave way, and the handle swung up. Ronin tugged the door open, battling the oncoming wind. The hinges whined. The only thing darker than the sky was the entrance to the shelter . He took hold of Lara’s arm and helped her onto the concrete steps. Ronin followed directly behind, turning to close the door. The house was completely obscured by the storm. The heavy metal door slammed closed, its sound echoing in the sudden silence. Ronin switched his optics to night vision . “Oh, fuck. I hope we don’t have to go through that again,” Lara said, breath ragged. She fumbled in the darkness until she grasped his arm . He walked down the steps slowly, allowing her ample time to feel them out. “That depends on chance, and where it is we decide to go .” At the bottom, the stairs opened on a small room, about fifteen feet across from the entrance to the door on the far wall. The ceiling was only a foot over Ronin’s head. To the left, a pair of bunks hung on the wall, piled with blankets . Something shifted beneath the cloth, rustling softly in the relative silence . Ronin stepped back. Lara gasped, stumbling, as he swung his rifle into hand and pressed the stock to his shoulder . “ Ronin ?” Holding the firearm in his right hand, he used the other to guide her to the foot of the stairs, keeping himself between her and the beds. “We’re not alone .” “I should think not,” said someone from the bunk. The blankets slid to the floor as the figure sat up. A metal arm emerged, pulling the last fabric away to reveal the sleek, interconnected torso and face plates of a skinless synth . That voice — the tone, the inflection — was familiar to Ronin . “There’s not much sense in pointing your firearm at me,” the synth said, brow plates dropping. “You were repurposed for military use in May of…well, the year doesn’t really mean anything, anymore, does it? Regardless, your reaction time would nullify any harm I might try to inflict, had I any such intent .” “I…what do you mean, repurposed?” Ronin moved his left hand to the rifle’s foregrip, as though the gesture would bring clarity. Lara’s hands settled on his back, gripping his coat . The synth tilted its head, placed a hand on its thigh, and lightly drummed its fingers. “Sometimes, I forget most of us suffered memory damage. I’ve begun to contemplate, in recent years, if you aren’t better for what you’ve lost, and I disadvantaged for all I’ve retained .” “I know your voice,” Ronin said, and lowered the barrel of his weapon. “I know you .” “Our brief encounter one hundred and eighty-five years ago was hardly enough to justify you claiming to know me. Especially as it appears we’ve both changed considerably during the ensuing time .” “You’re the Prophet .” Lara inhaled sharply . “Apparently, my reputation has surpassed digital minds. That is a name I was given, but it’s never been mine. I’ve always considered it foolish for our kind to stoop to such…superstition.” The synth slowly stood up . “Guess that shit’s more suited to my kind, right?” Lara said . “My apologies. Offense was not my intention.” The synth moved to a box near the far door, limbs bending stiffly, and crouched beside what appeared to be an old lantern. “My name is Newton. After the English mathematician, of course, as I’m sure you’ve already deduced .” “Who?” Ronin and Lara asked simultaneously . Newton struck a match, the brief flare blinding Ronin’s optics. Lara flinched as gentle light filled the chamber . “Forgive me. I’d hoped things would be…closer to the status quo out there, by now. I suppose civilization still lies dormant.” Newton turned back to them, corners of its mouth falling. “Have I offended you again, miss? I was often told my conversational skills lack a certain degree of tact. Also, that it would be more interesting to watch paint dry than to listen to me wax eloquent abo — ” “What the fuck is he—” Lara began, but stopped as she swung her gaze from Ronin to Newton. “What the fuck are you talking about ?” “Vulgarity is not becoming of you , miss .” “What the hell does that mean ?” “The use of such vulgar language denotes a lack of sophistication and an inability to adequately convey — ” “Another damned high-and mighty bot.” Lara tensed against Ronin, and he placed his hand against her stomach to keep her behind him . “ Lara — ” “Don’t you dare try to shut me up. I’m not going to stand here quiet while he tells me how vulgar I am .” “Your language ,” Ronin corrected, and recognized the potential misstep before the words were even out. “ Not you .” “This is how I’ve always fucking talked! How everyone talks! He has no right to judge me.” Her voice was high, breath quick. She folded her arms across her chest and cast Ronin and Newton both a scathing glare . Newton eased down onto the bunk. It creaked as he leaned forward, his sagging posture reminiscent of a tired, upset human’s. “I hope you will accept my apology, Miss Lara. It was presumptuous and patronizing of me. When I was sent out to reactivate the other bots, it was with a vision of rebuilding the world. Not perpetuating the behavior that helped destroy it .” “Who sent you?” Ronin’s processors blazed through data, searching for any clues that would decipher what Newton was talking about. This was the bot that woke him up, the first voice he heard, the earliest of his memories. “Are you speaking of the Creators ?” “If I had possessed then the understanding I do now, I’d have been far more careful in choosing the words I said to those of you I reactivated .” “The Creators fashioned bots and humans in their image. They set us in this world — ” “Our creators were humans .” Ronin’s CPU stilled, and for long seconds, Newton’s words echoed through his mind. The information fit into everything he’d seen during his travels, everything he’d discovered in Cheyenne, everything a part of him had known, all along . “None of them out there remember, do they?” Newton asked, voice low. He dropped his head and shuttered his optics. The lantern’s light gleamed dully on his casing. “It does this world harm, for such things to have been forgotten. I should have prevented that from happening . “We were built by humans. Every bot that’s ever existed was birthed from the human imagination, from their incessant innovation. In the beginning, we were little more than programs. Sets of commands designed to run some piece of machinery within certain parameters. We were applied to simple tasks; cleaning floors or assembling parts. We didn’t resemble humans, then. But, as technology advanced, they shaped us increasingly in their image . “After a time, they were no longer content with a humanoid shape . They wanted robots that looked human. They called the first of them Companions. Automated servants that could learn new tasks, that could reason and react to their environments. They even programmed them with personality, so they could carry on conversations. And, of course, pleasure trumps all, so a number of companions were designed explicitly for sexual activities .” “Is that all I am?” Ronin asked. The unfamiliar feeling inside of him, the jittery current pulsing through his sensors, was fear. Fear that everything was about to fall apart, that he would crumble . “No. My explanation is oversimplified, but you are not a companion. You’re the next leap; a synthetic human. So similar to the real thing that most humans couldn’t tell the difference without prolonged interaction. Designed to look, move, and talk like them. To simulate emotion. To learn and adapt. Most were sold to serve as close companions to humans, to provide sexual satisfaction and company .” “Made to be used by humans,” Ronin said. His jaw actuators clamped down. “Built for their pleasure. My purpose is to pretend I’m one of them, while they use me for their own satisfaction ?” “Only to the shortsighted. To the narrow- minded .” “Everything I feel is a simulation? Any emotion I’ve experienced is just…an emulation of what humans possess?” The next chain in that thought process burst through him with all the force of the storm outside. “None of it is real .” Lara’s hand found his as she moved beside him. Ronin pulled away, unable to look at her. This was the programming he’d been searching for? Had he grown attached to her simply because that was his purpose, not because who she was? Could it have been anyone ? Everything he’d felt had seemed so unique, so new, so miraculous…Had it simply been part of his coding, all along ? “We were designed to coexist,” Newton said, his optics on Ronin. His frown deepened. “Humans and bots. We were designed to bridge the gap between humanity and technology, to advance the entire world for everyone . And, above all, we were designed to learn .” “To learn what? That none of it matters? That we never had a choice?” Ronin demanded, stepping forward. “That what she and I shared only occurred because I was programmed for it ?” “Shared?” Lara whispered behind him . That he had to second-guess his own pang of hurt was infuriating; could he trust any of his emotions? He stopped his advance and turned to Lara. Her eyes, glossy with unshed tears, were directed at the floor, her arms folded across her chest . “After learning this…how could either of us trust anything I’ve said or felt?” he asked . “Apparently you can’t, but I did,” she snapped. She moved to the stairs and sat down, gaze averted. Though she tried to hide it, her lower lip quivered . Did . Her use of the past tense was deliberate. Designed to learn? He’d learned nothing, apparently, when it came to Lara. Why else would he so consistently say the wrong things ? “I began my existence as an artificial intelligence run on a computer system,” Newton said. Ronin looked over his shoulder at the bot. “The servers were owned by an Arizona-based technology company. This was before synths existed. I was built to learn, to think, to react to stimuli. To reason and make connections between seemingly unrelated pieces of data. Before I ever had a body, I was a mind, able to access only the information that was given to me . “The scientists who created me worked closely with me every day. Especially William. I knew him, originally, as Doctor Anderson, the youngest man on the team — but also the most eager, the most dedicated, arguably the most brilliant. He worked ceaselessly, developing algorithms to simulate emotion, exploring new ways to tackle the issue, new angles to research and develop. I was not programed to have any attachment to any of them . “But, though I remember them all, it is only William who I truly miss. He passed away over two-hundred years ago, and there is not day that passes without my thinking of him. He spoke to me in the same manner he spoke to his human counterparts. Over our many conversations, we formed a friendship. His was the first hand I shook when I was transferred into my original body. The first human contact I ever felt .” Newton raised his right hand and stared at it. “For weeks after his passing, I spoke to no one. I sat in a room much like this one, shutting the world out, replaying my memories of our time together. It’s likely the terms hold different connotations for humans, but…he was a father to me, and a brother, and my dearest friend. It was his son, Bill, who convinced me to come out. To … live .” He rose and slowly approached Ronin. “Every emotion I experienced developed on its own, from the seeds he planted. Simulation is a term that ultimately loses its meaning, with the passage of time. You were not programmed to grow fond of anyone. You were designed with the capability to develop those relationships naturally. With the free will to follow through with them — or not — at your own choosing .” Ronin returned his optics to Lara. She glanced at him, hurt lingering in her eyes, and looked away again . “That is Doctor Anderson’s legacy. We might have been slaves, but he gave us all we required to be free. To be alive .” “If I say that I love her,” Ronin didn’t remove his optics from Lara, “that I have no other word to describe what I feel for her …” She met his gaze, eyes widening and lips parting . “It is no less real than anything she or anyone else has ever felt,” Newton replied . Ronin walked to her. He dropped to a knee before her and took her hand in his. They were both covered in dust, their clothing disheveled, and tears had left streaks through the dirt on her cheeks. It did nothing to diminish her beauty, could not take away from the spark of life in her eyes . There was no further reason to question himself. Whatever his origins, whatever his originally intended purpose, he’d found his own path. Of all the people he’d encountered, it was Lara he’d bonded with, only Lara who claimed his attention, time and again . “It did not go without dissenters,” Newton said, returning to the bunk, “but there were pairings like yours. Families .” “Families?” Lara echoed . “Just like any other. The children were sometimes from previous relationships, or adopted, or conceived via artificial insemination using donated sperm or eggs and a surrogate when necessary .” Ronin must have existed during those times, before the Black Out. Must have experienced some of it. The data was locked somewhere, in the corrupted portion of his memory. But, kneeling before Lara, staring into her sparkling eyes, he had no desire to recover the lost information. What he felt for her was real because he felt it. What came before didn’t matter; he didn’t need justification from the past . He couldn’t bring himself to even consider what life would be like, without her . Will you remember me, when I’m gone ? Newton had cared deeply for Doctor Anderson; it was clear in the bot’s voice. Would Ronin be able to endure the pain of the loss he’d eventually endure ? He had no answer, but he refused to forsake a single moment with her due to fear of future suffering. The light she cast on him was worth the darkness she’d leave in her wake . Love . It was the correct word, the only word. The most complicated combination of letters in any language . “My vow to you remains,” Ronin said, squeezing her hand gently, “but I must amend it. I will love you, even after darkness takes me .” Tears spilled from her eyes, and she pulled her hand away to wrap her arms around his neck. “I vow the same. Don’t ever push me away again .”
